title: Economic profile: North Okanagan–Shuswap, British Columbia - Canada.ca
content:
## Economic profile: North Okanagan–Shuswap, British Columbia
North Okanagan–Shuswap Footnote 1 has a population of 148,631, consisting of 27% seniors (65 and older), 59% working-age adults (15 to 64) and 14% children (under 15).
The key sectors of the economy in North Okanagan–Shuswap, which account for 40% of the jobs in the region, are
- health care and social assistance
There are 65,140 employed residents, of which 11% are immigrants. Footnote 2
The economy generates a total of $8.2 billion in household and business incomes, which amounts to $55,073 per resident.

### General age structure – 2021
% children (under 15)
% working-age adults (15 to 64)
% of seniors (65 and older)
In order to support local services such as health care and social programs and maintain a good standard of living for everyone in the region, it is essential to have more working-age adults than seniors. North Okanagan–Shuswap has fewer working-age adults than seniors compared to the average in British Columbia.
From 2011 to 2021, North Okanagan–Shuswap experienced
- a 13% increase in population, compared to a 14% increase in the province
- a 29% increase in the population’s proportion of seniors, compared to a 30% increase in the province
- an 8.8% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, compared to a 5.2% decrease in the province

### Population structure – Changes from 2011 to 2021
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 3 the region could experience Footnote 4
- a 6.6% decrease in population
- a 7.3% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, which could limit the availability and quality of local services
- a further increase in the population’s proportion of seniors by 29%, potentially changing the demand for local services, including health care and long-term care

### No immigration – Possible changes from 2021 to 2031
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
The region relies heavily on working-age adults to keep local services running smoothly and to maintain a good standard of living and economic growth. Immigrants can help by filling job gaps and bringing new skills and ideas, which keeps the local economy strong.
From 2011 to 2021, North Okanagan–Shuswap experienced
- a 9.8% increase in workforce Footnote 5 size, compared to a 13% increase in the province
- a 19% increase in the number of immigrant workers, compared to a 21% increase in the province
- a total of 14,644 retirements, Footnote 6 which amounts to 21% of the 2021 workforce, compared to 17% in the province
- a change in the average income per resident from local jobs relative to the province, which is 13% lower than the average in British Columbia in 2021

By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 8 the region could experience Footnote 9
- a 42% decrease in workforce size
- a 39% decrease in the number of jobs
- an increase in the proportion of seniors in the population from 27% to 35%

## Attracting skilled workers
North Okanagan–Shuswap is one of 14 communities in Canada participating in the Rural Community Immigration Pilot , a community-driven pilot designed to spread the benefits of economic immigration to smaller communities.
Unless otherwise noted, all statistics are from the 2011, 2016 or 2021 editions of the Statistics Canada Census, and all calculations are from the Canadian Centre for Economic Analysis.
